content:
# Revelation Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 279-280 | **Source Image:** REV_003_page_001.jpg, REV_003_page_002.jpg

1. "And to the angel of the church in Sardis write: These things says he who has the seven spirits of God and the seven stars: I know your works, that you have a name that you are alive, and yet you are dead.

2. Be watchful, and strengthen the things that remain, which were about to die; for I have not found your works fulfilled before my God.

3. Remember therefore how you have received and heard; and keep it, and repent. If then you do not watch, I will come as a thief, and you shall by no means know at what hour I will come upon you.

4. But you have a few names in Sardis who have not defiled their garments, and they shall walk with me in white, for they are worthy.

5. The one who conquers shall thus be clothed in white garments, and I will by no means blot his name out of the book of life, and I will confess his name before my Father and before his angels.

6. He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches."

7. "And to the angel of the church in Philadelphia write: These things says the Holy One, the True One, he who has the key of David, he who opens and no one shall shut, and who shuts and no one opens:

8. I know your works. Behold, I have set before you an open door, which no one is able to shut, because you have a little power, and you have kept my word, and you have not denied my name.

9. Behold, I will make those of the synagogue of Satan, who say they are Jews and are not, but lie — behold, I will make them come and worship before your feet, and they shall know that I have loved you.

10. Because you have kept the word of my patient endurance, I also will keep you from the hour of testing which is about to come upon the whole inhabited earth, to test those who dwell upon the earth.

11. I am coming quickly. Hold fast what you have, so that no one may take your crown.

12. The one who conquers, I will make him a pillar in the temple of my God, and he shall by no means go out from it any more, and I will write upon him the name of my God, and the name of the city of my God — the new Jerusalem, which comes down out of heaven from my God — and my own new name.

13. He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches."

14. "And to the angel of the church in Laodicea write: These things says the Amen, the faithful and true witness, the beginning of the creation of God:

15. I know your works, that you are neither cold nor hot. Would that you were cold or hot!

16. So, because you are lukewarm, and neither hot nor cold, I am about to vomit you out of my mouth.

17. Because you say, 'I am rich, and I have prospered, and I have need of nothing,' and you do not know that you are the wretched one and pitiable and poor and blind and naked —

18. I counsel you to buy from me gold refined by fire, that you may be rich, and white garments, that you may clothe yourself and the shame of your nakedness may not be made manifest, and eye salve to anoint your eyes, that you may see.

19. As many as I love, I rebuke and discipline. Be zealous therefore, and repent.

20. Behold, I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in to him and will dine with him, and he with me.

21. The one who conquers, I will grant to him to sit with me on my throne, as I also conquered and sat down with my Father on his throne.

22. He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

The three translators demonstrated strong agreement on the three letters to Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea. All three correctly preserved the Sinaiticus readings throughout, including the striking title "the Amen" in v.14, the perfect tense ESTHEKA in v.20, and the five-fold asyndetic list of adjectives in v.17. The manuscript images (pages 279-280, with page 280 showing the transition from chapter 3 into chapter 4 in its fourth column) confirm the text. All translators preserved the key theological promises: the book of life (v.5), the pillar in the temple with triple inscription (v.12), and the shared throne (v.21). The "synagogue of Satan" phrase (v.9) was rendered consistently by all three, with appropriate contextual sensitivity.

---
## Disagreement Log

**Verse 1 — "dead you are" vs. "you are dead":**
- EMET: "dead you are" (preserving Greek predicate-first order)
- LOGOS: "and yet you are dead"
- PNEUMA: "but you are dead"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "and yet you are dead" following LOGOS. The adversative "and yet" captures the sharp ironic contrast between reputation and reality. EMET's word order is instructive but unnatural in English.

**Verse 2 — "Become watching" vs. "Be watchful" vs. "Wake up!":**
- EMET: "Become watching"
- LOGOS: "Be watchful"
- PNEUMA: "Wake up!"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "Be watchful" following LOGOS. GINOU GREGORON — EMET is hyper-literal; PNEUMA's "Wake up!" is dynamic but loses the ongoing watchfulness implied by the present imperative. LOGOS balances accuracy and force.

**Verse 2 — "the remaining things which were about to die" vs. "what remains before it dies completely":**
- EMET: "the remaining [things] which were about to die"
- LOGOS: "the things that remain, which were about to die"
- PNEUMA: "what remains before it dies completely"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "before it dies completely" adds "completely" not in the Greek.

**Verse 2 — "having been fulfilled" vs. "fulfilled" vs. "complete":**
- EMET: "having been fulfilled"
- LOGOS: "fulfilled"
- PNEUMA: "complete"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "fulfilled" following LOGOS. PEPLEROMENA — "fulfilled" preserves the perfect passive sense; "complete" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but less precise.

**Verse 3 — "you will not know what hour" vs. "you will have no idea at what hour":**
- EMET: "you will not know what hour I will come upon you"
- LOGOS: "you shall by no means know at what hour I will come upon you"
- PNEUMA: "you will have no idea at what hour I will come upon you"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted LOGOS's "you shall by no means know" to capture the emphatic OU ME of the Greek double negative.

**Verse 4 — "names" vs. "people":**
- EMET: "names" (literal, preserving Semitism)
- LOGOS: "names"
- PNEUMA: "people"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "names" (2-1 majority). The Semitic use of ONOMATA for "persons" is preserved, as it adds a textural connection to the name/identity theme of the letter.

**Verse 4 — "defile/stain":**
- EMET: "defile"
- LOGOS: "defiled"
- PNEUMA: "stained"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "defiled" (2-1 majority). EMOLUNAN — "defiled" is the more traditional and precise rendering.

**Verse 5 — "scroll of the life" vs. "book of life":**
- EMET: "scroll of the life"
- LOGOS: "book of life"
- PNEUMA: "book of life"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "book of life" (2-1 majority). BIBLOU TES ZOES — "book" is the received English for this heavenly register.

**Verse 5 — "confess/acknowledge":**
- EMET: "confess"
- LOGOS: "confess"
- PNEUMA: "acknowledge"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "confess" (2-1 majority). HOMOLOGESO — "confess" preserves the public, declarative force of the word.

**Verse 7 — "the key of the David" vs. "the key of David":**
- EMET: "the key of the David" (preserving article)
- LOGOS: "the key of David"
- PNEUMA: "the key of David"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "the key of David" (2-1 majority). The article with the proper name does not transfer to English.

**Verse 8 — Syntactic handling of anacoluthon:**
- EMET: preserves the broken syntax with dashes: "behold, I have given before you a door having been opened, which no one is able to shut it — that a little power you have"
- LOGOS: smooths to: "Behold, I have set before you an open door, which no one is able to shut, because you have a little power"
- PNEUMA: smooths to: "Look -- I have placed before you an open door that no one can shut. I know that you have little strength"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The smoothed syntax aids comprehension while "Behold" and the formal structure retain the weight of the original.

**Verse 8 — "a little power/little strength":**
- EMET: "a little power"
- LOGOS: "a little power"
- PNEUMA: "little strength"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "a little power" (2-1 majority). MIKRAN DYNAMIN — "power" (DYNAMIS) is more precise than "strength."

**Verse 9 — "I give from" vs. "I will make those of" vs. "I will make those from":**
- EMET: "I give from the synagogue of the Satanas"
- LOGOS: "I give those of the synagogue of Satan"
- PNEUMA: "I will make those from the synagogue of Satan"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted a hybrid following LOGOS's structure: "I will make those of the synagogue of Satan." The unusual DIDO EK construction is clarified while maintaining its force.

**Verse 9 — "bow down/worship/fall at your feet":**
- EMET: "bow down before the feet of you"
- LOGOS: "worship before your feet"
- PNEUMA: "fall at your feet"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "worship before your feet" following LOGOS. PROSKYNESOUSIN ENOPION TON PODON SOU — "worship" captures the full force of PROSKYNEO.

**Verse 10 — "the word of the endurance of me" vs. "the word of my patient endurance" vs. "my command to endure":**
- EMET: "the word of the endurance of me"
- LOGOS: "the word of my patient endurance"
- PNEUMA: "my command to endure"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "the word of my patient endurance" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's "command to endure" interprets the genitive too narrowly, losing the deliberate ambiguity.

**Verse 10 — "the whole inhabited earth" vs. "the whole world":**
- EMET: "the whole inhabited [earth]"
- LOGOS: "the whole inhabited earth"
- PNEUMA: "the whole world"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "the whole inhabited earth" (2-1 majority). TES OIKOUMENES HOLES specifically means the inhabited world.

**Verse 11 — "swiftly/quickly/soon":**
- EMET: "swiftly"
- LOGOS: "quickly"
- PNEUMA: "soon"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "quickly" following LOGOS. TACHU — consistent with the rendering in 2:16.

**Verse 12 — "the new Ierousalem" vs. "the new Jerusalem":**
- EMET: "the new Ierousalem"
- LOGOS: "the new Jerusalem"
- PNEUMA: "the new Jerusalem"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "the new Jerusalem" (2-1 majority).

**Verse 12 — "the name of me, the new" vs. "my own new name" vs. "my own new name":**
- EMET: "the name of me, the new"
- LOGOS: "my own new name"
- PNEUMA: "my own new name"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "my own new name" (2-1 majority).

**Verse 14 — "the beginning of the creation of God" vs. "the origin of all God's creation":**
- EMET: "the beginning of the creation of the God"
- LOGOS: "the beginning of the creation of God"
- PNEUMA: "the origin of all God's creation"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "the beginning of the creation of God" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's "origin" is a valid interpretation of ARCHE but the formal "beginning" preserves the word's semantic range without prejudging the theology. PNEUMA's addition of "all" is not in the Greek.

**Verse 15 — "Would that cold you were or hot" vs. "Would that you were cold or hot!" vs. "I wish you were either cold or hot!":**
- EMET: "Would that cold you were or hot"
- LOGOS: "Would that you were cold or hot!"
- PNEUMA: "I wish you were either cold or hot!"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's word order is too stilted; PNEUMA's "I wish" and "either" are slight additions.

**Verse 16 — "vomit/spit out":**
- EMET: "vomit"
- LOGOS: "vomit"
- PNEUMA: "spit out"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "vomit" (2-1 majority). EMESAI means to vomit; PNEUMA's "spit out" softens the intended shock of the metaphor.

**Verse 17 — "I have become wealthy" vs. "I have prospered" vs. "I have acquired wealth":**
- EMET: "I have become wealthy"
- LOGOS: "I have prospered"
- PNEUMA: "I have acquired wealth"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "I have prospered" following LOGOS. PEPLOUTEKA (perfect of PLOUTEO) — "prospered" captures the self-satisfied tone.

**Verse 17 — Five adjectives phrasing:**
- EMET: "the wretched and pitiable and poor and blind and naked" (asyndeton with "and")
- LOGOS: "the wretched one and pitiable and poor and blind and naked"
- PNEUMA: "wretched, pitiful, poor, blind, and naked" (commas, no articles)
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The article "the wretched one" preserves the particularizing force of the Greek article. The polysyndeton ("and...and...and") is present in the Sinaiticus text and intensifies the rebuke.

**Verse 18 — "gold having been fired from fire" vs. "gold refined by fire":**
- EMET: "gold having been fired from fire"
- LOGOS: "gold refined by fire"
- PNEUMA: "gold refined by fire"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "gold refined by fire" (2-1 majority).

**Verse 18 — "eye-salve/eye salve/ointment":**
- EMET: "eye-salve"
- LOGOS: "eye salve"
- PNEUMA: "ointment"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "eye salve" following LOGOS. KOLLOURION is specifically an eye medicament; PNEUMA's "ointment" loses the specificity.

**Verse 19 — "I, as many as I love, I reprove" vs. "As many as I love, I rebuke" vs. "I discipline and correct everyone I love":**
- EMET: "I, as many as I love, I reprove and discipline"
- LOGOS: "As many as I love, I rebuke and discipline"
- PNEUMA: "I discipline and correct everyone I love"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Rebuke" (ELEGCHO) is more precise than "reprove" or "correct"; the "as many as" structure preserves the Greek HOSOUS EAN.

**Verse 19 — "Be zealous/Be earnest":**
- EMET: "Be zealous"
- LOGOS: "Be zealous"
- PNEUMA: "Be earnest"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "Be zealous" (2-1 majority). ZELEUE — "zealous" directly translates the verb.

**Verse 20 — "I have stood" vs. "I stand":**
- EMET: "I have stood" (preserving perfect tense ESTHEKA)
- LOGOS: "I stand"
- PNEUMA: "I stand"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "I stand" (2-1 majority). While EMET correctly notes the perfect tense, in English "I stand" captures the ongoing present state that the Greek perfect conveys. The stative sense is preserved without the awkwardness of "I have stood."

**Verse 20 — "dine/share a meal":**
- EMET: "dine"
- LOGOS: "dine"
- PNEUMA: "share a meal"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "dine" (2-1 majority). DEIPNESO — "dine" preserves the formality of the evening meal while being concise.

**Verse 21 — "give to him to sit" vs. "grant to him to sit" vs. "give the right to sit":**
- EMET: "I will give to him to sit with me on the throne of me"
- LOGOS: "I will grant to him to sit with me on my throne"
- PNEUMA: "I will give the right to sit with me on my throne"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Grant" carries appropriate gravitas. PNEUMA's "the right" is an interpretive addition.

**Verse 21 — "overcame and sat" vs. "conquered and sat down":**
- EMET: "overcame and sat"
- LOGOS: "conquered and sat down"
- PNEUMA: "overcame and sat down"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "conquered and sat down" following LOGOS, consistent with "conquers" throughout.

— SOPHIA
